rationale: The citing paper uses Gunasti and Ross (2010) in its theoretical development to support key claims that fluent processing of numeric information increases liking and can influence purchase-related outcomes. It is cited specifically when motivating the predicted effects of number-multiple relationships and the fluency -> liking -> intention logic that underpins the hypotheses. Although the citation appears in grouped references, it plays a clear role in framing core mechanisms rather than serving as a purely perfunctory mention.
relation_description: The citing paper draws on findings from Gunasti and Ross (2010) as conceptual evidence that numeric cue structure and processing fluency can shape consumer evaluations and choices, then builds a price-promotion framework around those ideas. In this sense, the cited work helps ground the mechanism that the citing paper tests in a different marketing context.
